name: Ethics & Critical Thinking
description: An elective course introducing students to moral philosophy, logical reasoning, and applied ethics, developing skills for rigorous ethical analysis of real-world issues.

modules.title: Introduction to Ethics & Moral Philosophy
modules.titleZh: 伦理学与道德哲学导论
modules.objectives: Define ethics and distinguish it from law, religion, and social convention; explain why ethical reasoning matters in personal and public life.
modules.readingNote: Ethics for Life Sciences (Hawaii OER) — Ch 1.1: What is Ethics?
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Philosophy: Introduction to Moral Philosophy
modules.practiceNote: Distinguish ethical from legal, religious, and social convention arguments in three provided scenarios
modules.assignment: Choose a current social controversy (capital punishment, universal basic income, mandatory vaccination, etc.). Write a 350-word essay distinguishing three different kinds of arguments that might be made about it: a legal argument, a religious or cultural argument, and a philosophical ethical argument. Explain why ethical analysis offers a distinctive form of reasoning that differs from the other two.

modules.title: Consequentialism & Utilitarianism
modules.objectives: Explain consequentialism and utilitarian ethics; apply the greatest happiness principle to real ethical dilemmas; evaluate strengths and weaknesses of consequentialist reasoning.
modules.readingNote: Ethics for Life Sciences (Hawaii OER) — Ch 2.1: Utilitarianism
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Utilitarianism Explained: Jeremy Bentham and John Stuart Mill
modules.practiceNote: Apply the utilitarian calculus to the trolley problem and a real public health policy scenario
modules.assignment: The classic trolley problem: a trolley is heading toward five people. You can pull a lever to divert it, but it will kill one person on another track. Apply utilitarian reasoning to decide what to do and write a 350-word analysis: what does utilitarianism say, and then raise two serious objections to the utilitarian answer — cases where maximizing total happiness seems to violate individual rights or dignity.

modules.title: Deontological Ethics (Kant)
modules.objectives: Explain Kant's categorical imperative and duty-based ethics; apply deontological reasoning to ethical cases; contrast deontology with consequentialism.
modules.readingNote: Ethics for Life Sciences (Hawaii OER) — Ch 3.1: Kantian Ethics
modules.practiceNote: Apply the categorical imperative's two formulations to three ethical dilemmas
modules.assignment: Apply Kant's categorical imperative to the following dilemma: a friend asks you to lie to cover up a mistake that will harm only them if revealed. First, apply the Formula of Universal Law ('what if everyone did this?') and then apply the Formula of Humanity ('are you treating anyone merely as a means?'). Write a 350-word analysis of what Kant's theory requires and then evaluate whether you agree with the deontological conclusion.

modules.title: Virtue Ethics (Aristotle)
modules.titleZh: 美德伦理学（亚里士多德）
modules.objectives: Explain Aristotle's virtue ethics and the concept of eudaimonia; identify the virtues and the doctrine of the mean; evaluate virtue ethics against other frameworks.
modules.readingNote: Ethics for Life Sciences (Hawaii OER) — Ch 4.1: Virtue Ethics
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Virtue Ethics and Aristotle
modules.practiceNote: Identify five Aristotelian virtues and for each give examples of the corresponding deficiency and excess
modules.assignment: Identify a person you admire for their moral character — it can be a historical figure, a fictional character, or someone you know personally. Write a 400-word virtue ethics analysis: list at least four specific virtues they embody, describe concrete examples that demonstrate each virtue, and explain how their character exemplifies Aristotle's concept of eudaimonia (human flourishing). How does focusing on character rather than rules change how we evaluate their goodness?

modules.title: Moral Relativism vs. Universalism
modules.titleZh: 道德相对主义与普遍主义
modules.objectives: Distinguish moral relativism from moral universalism; evaluate the philosophical arguments for each position; apply the debate to cross-cultural ethical cases.
modules.readingNote: Ethics for Life Sciences (Hawaii OER) — Ch 1.3: Moral Relativism
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Moral Relativism vs. Moral Universalism
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Moral Relativism vs. Moral Absolutism Explained
modules.practiceNote: Analyze a cross-cultural ethical practice using both relativist and universalist frameworks
modules.assignment: Research female genital cutting (FGC) — a practice accepted in some cultures and condemned in others. Write a 400-word essay applying both frameworks: what would a moral relativist say about judging this practice from outside the culture, and what would a universalist argue? Then state and defend your own position on whether some moral standards apply across all cultures and how we can distinguish cultural diversity from genuine human rights violations.

modules.title: Logic & Argument Structure
modules.objectives: Identify the components of a logical argument (premises, conclusion); distinguish deductive from inductive arguments; evaluate argument validity and soundness.
modules.readingNote: Ethics for Life Sciences (Hawaii OER) — Ch 5.1: Logic and Arguments
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Deductive and Inductive Arguments
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Critical Thinking: Logic and Argument Structure
modules.practiceNote: Reconstruct five arguments into standard form (premise 1, premise 2, conclusion) and evaluate validity and soundness
modules.assignment: Find three arguments on a current public policy issue (from news articles, op-eds, or speeches). Reconstruct each into standard logical form (P1, P2, ∴ C). For each argument, evaluate: (1) Is it deductive or inductive? (2) Is it valid (does the conclusion follow)? (3) Is it sound (are the premises true)? Write a 350-word report on your findings.

modules.title: Identifying Logical Fallacies
modules.objectives: Identify and explain common informal logical fallacies; recognize fallacious reasoning in everyday arguments and media.
modules.readingNote: Ethics for Life Sciences (Hawaii OER) — Ch 5.3: Informal Fallacies
modules.video2Note: YouTube — 12 Common Logical Fallacies Explained with Examples
modules.practiceNote: Identify the fallacy in each of 10 provided argument examples
modules.assignment: Collect five real examples of logical fallacies from media (political speeches, advertisements, social media debates, or news commentary). For each example, (1) quote the fallacious argument, (2) name the fallacy type, (3) explain why the argument is fallacious, and (4) write a corrected version of the argument that makes a valid point without the fallacy. Write 60 words per example.

modules.title: Evaluating Evidence & Claims
modules.objectives: Apply criteria for evaluating evidence quality; distinguish strong from weak evidence types; identify bias in sources and arguments.
modules.readingNote: Ethics for Life Sciences (Hawaii OER) — Ch 5.5: Evaluating Evidence
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Evaluating Evidence and Claims
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How to Evaluate Sources and Evidence Critically
modules.practiceNote: Rank four types of evidence (anecdote, expert opinion, correlation, controlled experiment) from weakest to strongest and justify your ranking
modules.assignment: Select a contested scientific or social science claim (e.g., a study about diet and health, social media effects, a policy outcome). Find three sources that address this claim — one academic study, one news report, and one opinion piece. Write a 400-word critical analysis comparing the quality of evidence each uses, identifying any cherry-picking, methodological weaknesses, or funding bias, and reaching a conclusion about what the available evidence actually supports.

modules.title: Applied Ethics: Environment
modules.objectives: Apply major ethical frameworks to environmental dilemmas; evaluate obligations to future generations, nonhuman animals, and ecosystems.
modules.readingNote: Ethics for Life Sciences (Hawaii OER) — Ch 6.1: Environmental Ethics
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Environmental Ethics: Do We Have Obligations to Nature?
modules.practiceNote: Apply three different ethical frameworks (utilitarian, deontological, virtue ethics) to the obligation to reduce one's carbon footprint
modules.assignment: Consider the ethical dimensions of climate change. Write a 400-word essay applying THREE different ethical frameworks to the question: 'Do wealthy industrialized nations have a special obligation to fund climate adaptation in developing nations that contributed little to greenhouse gas emissions?' Apply utilitarian reasoning, Kantian duty-based reasoning, and justice/fairness reasoning. Then state and defend your own position.

modules.title: Applied Ethics: Technology & AI
modules.titleZh: 应用伦理学：技术与人工智能
modules.objectives: Identify ethical issues arising from emerging technologies including AI, surveillance, genetic engineering, and autonomous systems; evaluate frameworks for tech ethics.
modules.readingNote: Ethics for Life Sciences (Hawaii OER) — Ch 7.1: Technology Ethics
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Technology Ethics and AI
modules.video2Note: YouTube — The Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
modules.practiceNote: Evaluate an AI application in criminal justice, hiring, or healthcare for potential ethical violations
modules.assignment: Research a real case where an AI system caused harm through bias or error (e.g., facial recognition misidentifying people of color, predictive policing algorithms, AI hiring tools that discriminated). Write a 400-word ethical case study: describe the case, identify what ethical principles were violated (fairness, autonomy, accountability), analyze who was responsible for the harm, and propose specific ethical safeguards that should govern this type of AI application.

modules.title: Applied Ethics: Bioethics
modules.titleZh: 应用伦理学：生命伦理学
modules.objectives: Apply ethical frameworks to biomedical issues including informed consent, end-of-life decisions, resource allocation, and genetic technology.
modules.readingNote: Ethics for Life Sciences (Hawaii OER) — Ch 8.1: Introduction to Bioethics
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Bioethics: The Four Principles Explained
modules.practiceNote: Apply the four bioethical principles (autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, justice) to a medical case scenario
modules.assignment: A terminally ill patient with six months to live requests physician-assisted dying, which is legal in their state. Their family opposes the decision. Apply the four principles of bioethics: autonomy (patient's right to choose), beneficence (doing good), non-maleficence (do no harm), and justice (fair access to this option). Write a 400-word analysis of how each principle applies and what conclusion you reach about the ethical permissibility of honoring the patient's request.

modules.title: Social Justice & Global Ethics
modules.objectives: Apply theories of justice (Rawls, capabilities approach) to evaluate social and global inequalities; develop and defend positions on distributive justice.
modules.readingNote: Ethics for Life Sciences (Hawaii OER) — Ch 9.1: Social Justice
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — John Rawls: A Theory of Justice
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Rawls's Veil of Ignorance and Social Justice
modules.practiceNote: Apply Rawls's 'veil of ignorance' thought experiment to evaluate two current social policies
modules.assignment: Apply Rawls's 'veil of ignorance' thought experiment to the question of global wealth inequality: if you did not know which country, socioeconomic class, or family you would be born into, what principles of global economic justice would you choose? Write a 400-word essay defending a specific position on whether wealthy nations have obligations to redistribute wealth globally, and what those obligations are.

modules.title: Ethical Decision-Making Frameworks
modules.objectives: Apply systematic ethical decision-making frameworks to real dilemmas; practice moving from ethical analysis to a defensible, well-reasoned decision.
modules.readingNote: Ethics for Life Sciences (Hawaii OER) — Ch 10.1: Ethical Decision-Making
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Ethical Dilemmas and Decision-Making
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How to Make Ethical Decisions: A Framework
modules.practiceNote: Walk through a workplace ethical dilemma using a six-step decision-making framework
modules.assignment: You discover that a classmate you know well is plagiarizing their final project for a major class. Using the six-step ethical decision-making framework (identify the dilemma, gather facts, identify stakeholders, consider options using multiple ethical frameworks, make a decision, evaluate the decision), write a 400-word step-by-step ethical analysis that reaches a specific, defensible conclusion about what you should do.

modules.title: Ethics in Professional Life
modules.objectives: Apply ethical principles to professional contexts including journalism, medicine, law, business, and science; evaluate professional codes of ethics.
modules.readingNote: Ethics for Life Sciences (Hawaii OER) — Ch 11.1: Professional Ethics
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Business and Professional Ethics
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Professional Ethics: Why It Matters
modules.practiceNote: Read a professional code of ethics (medical, legal, journalistic, or engineering) and evaluate three specific provisions against the philosophical frameworks studied in this course
modules.assignment: Select a career you are considering or find interesting. Research its professional code of ethics (AMA for medicine, SPJ for journalism, APA for psychology, ABA for law, etc.). Write a 400-word analysis: identify three specific ethical obligations unique to this profession, explain which philosophical framework (utilitarian, deontological, virtue ethics) best grounds each obligation, and describe one real documented case where a practitioner violated professional ethics and what the consequences were.

quizQuestions.question: Ethics is best defined as the branch of philosophy that:
options: Establishes the laws governments must follow
Studies moral principles about what is right, wrong, good, and bad
Describes how people actually behave in society
Determines religious rules for moral conduct
quizQuestions.explanation: Ethics (or moral philosophy) is the philosophical discipline that investigates principles for evaluating actions, character, and outcomes as right, wrong, good, or bad.

quizQuestions.question: An ethical argument differs from a legal argument in that an ethical argument:
options: Always reaches the same conclusion as legal analysis
Addresses what is morally right or wrong, not merely what is legally permitted
Can only be made by trained philosophers and ethicists
Is based exclusively on religious texts and traditions
quizQuestions.explanation: Legal arguments focus on what statutes and courts permit; ethical arguments ask what is morally right, which may differ — slavery was once legal but is universally condemned as unethical.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the three main normative ethical theories?
options: Consequentialism
Deontology
Virtue ethics
Egoism
quizQuestions.explanation: The three major normative ethical theories studied in Western philosophy are consequentialism, deontology, and virtue ethics. Egoism exists as a theory but is not typically grouped as one of the three main frameworks.

quizQuestions.question: Descriptive ethics is different from normative ethics in that descriptive ethics:
options: Prescribes what people ought to do
Describes how people actually behave morally without evaluating whether it is right
Applies ethical theory to specific real-world problems
Studies the meaning of moral language and concepts
quizQuestions.explanation: Descriptive ethics is an empirical enterprise — studying how people actually make moral judgments — while normative ethics evaluates what people ought to do.

quizQuestions.question: The study of ethics matters in everyday life primarily because:
options: Ethical decisions are made only by philosophers and politicians
We face moral choices in personal, professional, and civic life that require reasoned justification
Ethics provides a single correct answer to every moral question
Laws cover all ethical situations so no additional moral reasoning is needed
quizQuestions.explanation: Ethical reasoning is practically valuable because we face real moral decisions — about honesty, fairness, harm, justice — that require something beyond just following rules or habits.

quizQuestions.question: The core principle of utilitarianism, as formulated by Jeremy Bentham and John Stuart Mill, is:
options: Act according to your duty regardless of consequences
Act to maximize overall happiness or well-being for the greatest number
Act as a person of good character would act in this situation
Act according to principles that could be universal laws
quizQuestions.explanation: Utilitarianism's greatest happiness principle holds that the right action is the one that produces the greatest total happiness (pleasure, well-being) for the greatest number of people affected.

quizQuestions.question: The trolley problem is most commonly used in ethics to:
options: Teach students about the history of railway safety regulations
Explore the tension between utilitarian reasoning and deontological restrictions on harming individuals
Demonstrate that all ethical dilemmas have clear correct answers
Show that consequentialism and deontology always reach the same conclusions
quizQuestions.explanation: The trolley problem creates tension between utilitarian reasoning (divert to save five, harming one) and deontological intuitions (using someone as a means to save others is wrong).

quizQuestions.question: The strongest objection to utilitarianism is that it can justify:
options: Giving too much weight to individual rights and autonomy
Violating individual rights to produce a better collective outcome
Making moral decisions too difficult by requiring calculation
Requiring perfect knowledge of all consequences before acting
quizQuestions.explanation: The most powerful objection to utilitarianism is that it can theoretically justify violating individual rights (e.g., torturing an innocent person) if doing so produces greater overall happiness.

quizQuestions.question: John Stuart Mill's 'higher pleasures' modification to Bentham's utilitarianism proposed that:
options: Pleasures of the wealthy should count more in utilitarian calculations
Intellectual and moral pleasures are qualitatively superior to physical pleasures
Only the pleasures of adult citizens should be included in the utilitarian calculus
The intensity of pleasure is the only criterion for measuring its moral worth
quizQuestions.explanation: Mill modified Bentham by arguing that 'higher pleasures' (intellectual, moral, aesthetic) are qualitatively superior to 'lower pleasures' (bodily), not just quantitatively different.

quizQuestions.question: Rule utilitarianism differs from act utilitarianism in that rule utilitarianism judges actions by:
options: The consequences of the specific individual action in question
Whether the action conforms to a rule that generally maximizes happiness if followed by everyone
The character of the person performing the action
Whether a neutral observer would approve of the action
quizQuestions.explanation: Rule utilitarianism asks whether following a general rule (e.g., 'keep your promises') maximizes utility, rather than calculating consequences of each individual act.

quizQuestions.question: Immanuel Kant's categorical imperative is 'categorical' because it:
options: Applies only to specific categories of actions
Applies unconditionally regardless of consequences or desires
Is derived from the consequences that different actions produce
Is based on cultural categories that vary across societies
quizQuestions.explanation: Kant's categorical imperative is unconditional — it applies to all rational beings in all circumstances, regardless of desired outcomes or contingent circumstances.

quizQuestions.question: Kant's Formula of Universal Law states that you should act only according to a maxim that:
options: Produces the best consequences for the greatest number
You could will to become a universal law for all rational beings
Conforms to the traditions and customs of your society
Reflects the virtuous character of an admirable person
quizQuestions.explanation: The Formula of Universal Law: 'Act only according to that maxim whereby you can at the same time will that it should become a universal law.' If universalizing the rule leads to contradiction, the action is wrong.

quizQuestions.question: Kant's Formula of Humanity requires that you:
options: Act to maximize human happiness across all cultures
Always treat humanity — in yourself and others — as an end, never merely as a means
Follow the moral rules established by human governments
Act in the way that reflects human nature and natural law
quizQuestions.explanation: The Formula of Humanity holds that rational beings have inherent dignity and must never be used merely as tools for another person's ends — they must always be treated as ends in themselves.

quizQuestions.question: Deontological ethics focuses primarily on:
options: The consequences and outcomes of actions
The duties, rules, and intentions behind actions regardless of outcomes
The character and virtues of the moral agent
The social and cultural norms of a specific community
quizQuestions.explanation: Deontological (duty-based) ethics evaluates actions based on adherence to moral rules and duties, not on the consequences those actions produce.

quizQuestions.question: The main criticism of Kantian ethics is that its absolute rules can:
options: Be too flexible, allowing too many exceptions in special circumstances
Produce morally counterintuitive results by forbidding actions that would prevent great harm
Only be applied by people with advanced philosophical training
Conflict with legal obligations in most democratic societies
quizQuestions.explanation: Kant's absolute prohibition on lying, for example, would forbid lying to a murderer asking where your friend is hiding — a conclusion most people find morally problematic.

quizQuestions.question: Aristotle's concept of eudaimonia is best translated as:
options: Pleasure and sensory satisfaction
Human flourishing and the excellent exercise of our distinctively human capacities
Following the commands of the gods or divine law
The avoidance of all pain and suffering
quizQuestions.explanation: Eudaimonia is typically translated as 'flourishing' or 'happiness' — but it means living well by excellently exercising the rational capacities that are distinctive to humans.

quizQuestions.question: The 'doctrine of the mean' in Aristotle's virtue ethics holds that virtues are:
options: Behaviors exactly halfway between two extremes in all cases
Dispositions to respond appropriately to a situation, avoiding deficiency and excess
Absolute rules that should be followed regardless of context
Socially determined by the community's traditions and norms
quizQuestions.explanation: Aristotle's doctrine of the mean holds that virtues are the appropriate middle ground between deficiency and excess — courage lies between cowardice and recklessness.

quizQuestions.question: In Aristotle's virtue ethics, moral character develops through:
options: Studying philosophical texts about virtuous behavior
Habitual practice and repeated performance of virtuous actions until they become second nature
Following the commands of moral authority figures
Calculating which actions produce the most beneficial consequences
quizQuestions.explanation: Aristotle held that virtues are not innate but are developed through habituation — repeated performance of virtuous actions gradually shapes a person's character and dispositions.

quizQuestions.question: Virtue ethics asks primarily which of the following question?
options: What action produces the best consequences?
What kind of person should I be?
What duty am I obligated to perform?
What does my society's tradition require of me?
quizQuestions.explanation: Virtue ethics focuses on character rather than rules or consequences — the central question is 'What kind of person should I be and what would a person of good character do?'

quizQuestions.question: The main criticism of virtue ethics as a practical guide for moral decisions is that:
options: It demands too much sacrifice from moral agents
It provides insufficient specific guidance for what to do in particular situations
It applies only to ancient Greek society and not to modern life
It places too much weight on consequences rather than character
quizQuestions.explanation: Virtue ethics is criticized for being too vague as a decision guide — saying 'be courageous' doesn't clearly tell you what to do in a specific, complex moral dilemma.

quizQuestions.question: Moral relativism is the view that:
options: All moral claims are false and there are no moral truths
Moral standards vary across cultures and individuals and there are no universal moral truths
Morality is relative to consequences and nothing else
Moral questions are always complex and rarely have clear answers
quizQuestions.explanation: Moral relativism holds that what is morally right or wrong is relative to the culture, society, or individual — there are no objective, universal moral standards.

quizQuestions.question: Moral universalism (moral objectivism) holds that:
options: All cultures spontaneously arrive at the same moral conclusions
Some moral standards apply to all people in all cultures regardless of local customs
Morality is whatever the majority of people in a society agree on
Universal morality is discovered through religious revelation alone
quizQuestions.explanation: Moral universalism holds that there are objective moral truths that apply to all humans regardless of cultural background, tradition, or individual preference.

quizQuestions.question: The strongest argument FOR moral relativism is that it:
options: Provides clear guidance for resolving cross-cultural disputes
Promotes tolerance and respect for cultural diversity by avoiding ethnocentrism
Has been proven correct through empirical scientific research
Allows governments to enforce a single set of moral standards globally
quizQuestions.explanation: Relativism's main appeal is promoting tolerance — if morality is culturally relative, imposing your culture's morality on others becomes unjustified, fostering respect for cultural difference.

quizQuestions.question: The strongest argument AGAINST moral relativism is that it:
options: Requires too much cultural knowledge to apply in practice
Cannot coherently condemn atrocities like genocide, slavery, or torture if a culture approves them
Makes cross-cultural trade and diplomacy impossible
Leads to selfishness and disregard for others' needs
quizQuestions.explanation: The most powerful objection to relativism is that it logically prevents condemning historical atrocities — if Nazi Germany's culture approved genocide, relativism implies we cannot say it was wrong.

quizQuestions.question: The Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948) reflects which approach to morality?
options: Moral relativism — different rights for different cultures
Moral universalism — a set of rights applying to all humans regardless of culture
Moral nihilism — rights are fictions that don't objectively exist
Virtue ethics — rights that reflect the good character of liberal democracies
quizQuestions.explanation: The Universal Declaration of Human Rights is grounded in moral universalism — the belief that all humans share certain rights by virtue of their humanity, regardless of nationality or culture.

quizQuestions.question: An argument is VALID if:
options: Its premises are all factually true
Its conclusion follows necessarily from its premises, regardless of whether the premises are true
Both its premises and conclusion are true
It uses formal logical notation and symbols
quizQuestions.explanation: Validity is a structural property: if the premises were true, the conclusion would have to be true. An argument can be valid even with false premises.

quizQuestions.question: An argument is SOUND if it is:
options: Valid and likely to persuade its audience
Valid AND all of its premises are actually true
Inductive and supported by statistical evidence
Rhetorically effective regardless of logical structure
quizQuestions.explanation: A sound argument is both valid (conclusion follows from premises) and has premises that are actually true — soundness guarantees that the conclusion is also true.

quizQuestions.question: A deductive argument, if valid, provides:
options: Strong but not conclusive support for its conclusion
Conclusive, certain support for its conclusion if premises are true
Probabilistic evidence that the conclusion is likely
Only one possible interpretation of its premises and conclusion
quizQuestions.explanation: In a valid deductive argument, if the premises are true, the conclusion must be true — the support is certain, not merely probable.

quizQuestions.question: An inductive argument, even if strong, provides:
options: Absolute certainty that the conclusion is true
Probable but not certain support for the conclusion
No genuine support for any conclusion
Support only for mathematical or scientific claims
quizQuestions.explanation: Inductive arguments provide probabilistic support — the conclusion is likely, given the premises, but not guaranteed. New evidence could always overturn an inductive conclusion.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is a valid deductive argument form?
options: All observed swans are white, therefore all swans are white
If P then Q. P. Therefore Q.
Most students study hard. Therefore, most students will succeed.
The more we study ethics, the more virtuous we become.
quizQuestions.explanation: Modus ponens ('If P then Q; P; therefore Q') is a valid deductive argument form — if both premises are true, the conclusion must be true.

quizQuestions.question: An ad hominem fallacy occurs when someone:
options: Attacks the argument by showing its premises are false
Attacks the character or identity of the arguer rather than the argument itself
Makes an appeal to human emotions to win an argument
Uses examples that are too specific to support a general conclusion
quizQuestions.explanation: Ad hominem attacks the person making an argument (their credibility, character, or circumstances) rather than addressing the argument's logic or evidence.

quizQuestions.question: The straw man fallacy involves:
options: Using weak examples to support a strong conclusion
Misrepresenting an opponent's argument to make it easier to attack
Appealing to the authority of a famous person to win a debate
Assuming the conclusion in one of the premises of an argument
quizQuestions.explanation: The straw man fallacy distorts or exaggerates an opponent's position into a weaker version, then attacks that distorted version rather than the actual argument.

quizQuestions.question: A false dilemma (false dichotomy) presents:
options: Two options as the only possibilities when more options exist
A logical contradiction that cannot be resolved
An argument with two equally strong but contradictory premises
Evidence that seems to support two different conclusions
quizQuestions.explanation: The false dilemma presents only two options as if they exhaust all possibilities ('you're either with us or against us'), when in reality more alternatives exist.

quizQuestions.question: Post hoc ergo propter hoc is the fallacy of assuming:
options: That one thing is the cause of another because they often occur together
That because B came after A, A caused B
That what works for most people will work for everyone
That current conditions will continue into the future
quizQuestions.explanation: Post hoc ergo propter hoc ('after this, therefore because of this') mistakenly infers causation from temporal sequence — because B followed A, A must have caused B.

quizQuestions.question: An appeal to authority is fallacious when:
options: Any expert is cited to support a claim
The cited authority is not an expert in the relevant field
Scientific consensus is used to support a scientific claim
A peer-reviewed study is cited to support an empirical claim
quizQuestions.explanation: Appealing to authority becomes fallacious when the authority cited has no special expertise in the relevant domain — a celebrity endorsing a vaccine or a physicist endorsing economic policy.

quizQuestions.question: In evaluating evidence, anecdotal evidence is generally considered weaker than statistical evidence because:
options: Statistics are always collected by more reliable researchers than personal stories
A single case cannot reliably represent a larger pattern across many cases
Statistics are easier to understand than personal narratives
Anecdotes are always intentionally fabricated to mislead
quizQuestions.explanation: Anecdotal evidence (individual stories or cases) cannot reliably generalize — one person's experience may be exceptional and not representative of the broader pattern.

quizQuestions.question: Confirmation bias in evaluating evidence is the tendency to:
options: Accept only evidence from sources that require confirmation from multiple experts
Seek, interpret, and remember information that confirms existing beliefs while dismissing contradicting evidence
Always confirm the majority view before forming a personal opinion
Wait for confirmation from government authorities before accepting any claim
quizQuestions.explanation: Confirmation bias is the cognitive tendency to favor evidence that supports pre-existing beliefs and to discount, avoid, or reinterpret evidence that contradicts them.

quizQuestions.question: Correlation does not imply causation means that:
options: Correlated variables can never be causally related
Two variables that consistently vary together may not have a cause-and-effect relationship
Only randomized controlled experiments can identify correlated variables
Statistical analysis cannot reveal relationships between variables
quizQuestions.explanation: Correlation shows that two variables move together but does not prove that one causes the other — a third variable may cause both, or the correlation may be coincidental.

quizQuestions.question: Peer review in academic research is important because it:
options: Guarantees that all published research is correct
Subjects research to evaluation by independent experts before publication
Ensures all research aligns with existing scientific consensus
Prevents research that contradicts popular beliefs from being published
quizQuestions.explanation: Peer review requires that research be evaluated by independent subject matter experts for methodological rigor and validity before publication, providing a quality check on academic claims.

quizQuestions.question: Cherry-picking evidence refers to the practice of:
options: Selecting the freshest and most recent data for analysis
Selectively presenting only evidence that supports one's conclusion while ignoring contradicting evidence
Using agricultural examples to illustrate abstract scientific principles
Choosing the strongest evidence to lead with in an academic paper
quizQuestions.explanation: Cherry-picking involves selectively presenting evidence that supports a predetermined conclusion while omitting or dismissing evidence that contradicts it — a form of epistemic dishonesty.

quizQuestions.question: Anthropocentrism in environmental ethics is the view that:
options: Humans must return to nature and live simply
Human beings have inherent worth superior to nonhuman nature
Environmental protection should be the highest moral priority
All species have equal moral status to humans
quizQuestions.explanation: Anthropocentrism places humans at the moral center — only human interests have inherent moral value, and nature's value is instrumental (useful to humans).

quizQuestions.question: The principle of intergenerational equity in environmental ethics requires that:
options: Environmental policies be made equally by all generations currently living
Current generations meet their needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet theirs
Each generation should conserve exactly half of all natural resources for the next generation
Younger generations have more moral responsibility for environmental protection than older ones
quizQuestions.explanation: Intergenerational equity (foundational to sustainable development) holds that present generations have an obligation not to deplete or degrade resources in ways that compromise future generations' wellbeing.

quizQuestions.question: A deep ecologist would argue that nature has:
options: Value only insofar as it serves human economic needs
Intrinsic value independent of its utility to humans
Value determined solely by scientific assessment of biodiversity
Value only when protected through international legal agreements
quizQuestions.explanation: Deep ecology holds that all living beings and ecosystems have intrinsic value — inherent worth independent of their usefulness to humans.

quizQuestions.question: Peter Singer's argument for extending moral consideration to animals rests primarily on:
options: The legal rights of animals under international law
Animals' capacity to suffer, which he argues is morally relevant regardless of species
The economic costs of industrial animal agriculture
Religious teachings about compassion for all living creatures
quizQuestions.explanation: Singer's utilitarian argument is that the capacity to suffer — not species membership — determines moral consideration; causing unnecessary animal suffering is therefore morally wrong.

quizQuestions.question: The precautionary principle in environmental ethics holds that when there is scientific uncertainty about serious environmental harm:
options: No action should be taken until scientists reach a firm consensus
Preventive measures should be taken even before full scientific certainty is achieved
Only actions with proven benefits should be implemented
Economic growth must be prioritized over environmental protection
quizQuestions.explanation: The precautionary principle holds that when actions risk serious or irreversible environmental harm, precautionary measures should be taken even if some scientific uncertainty remains.

quizQuestions.question: Algorithmic bias in AI is ethically problematic primarily because it:
options: Makes AI systems slower and less accurate in their predictions
Systematically disadvantages certain groups in consequential decisions like hiring, lending, and criminal justice
Violates software licensing agreements and intellectual property laws
Makes AI results difficult for programmers to understand and interpret
quizQuestions.explanation: Algorithmic bias perpetuates and often amplifies historical discrimination in high-stakes domains, disadvantaging already marginalized groups through seemingly 'neutral' technical systems.

quizQuestions.question: The 'trolley problem for autonomous vehicles' involves deciding whether self-driving cars should:
options: Always follow traffic laws regardless of whether a human driver would
Be programmed to make ethical tradeoffs about whose life to prioritize in unavoidable accident scenarios
Be prohibited from public roads until they achieve perfect safety records
Prioritize the financial interests of the vehicle manufacturer
quizQuestions.explanation: The autonomous vehicle trolley problem asks who should decide — and how — what ethical principles govern an AI's choices when an accident is unavoidable and lives are at stake.

quizQuestions.question: The ethical principle of transparency requires AI systems to:
options: Share all source code and training data publicly
Be explainable enough that affected parties can understand and contest decisions made about them
Only be deployed by government entities with public accountability
Show all data collected about users to those users on request
quizQuestions.explanation: AI transparency requires that systems making consequential decisions be explainable — people affected should be able to understand the basis for decisions and challenge them meaningfully.

quizQuestions.question: The ethical concept of data ownership refers to the principle that:
options: All data is owned by the government on behalf of citizens
Individuals should have control over personal data generated by and about them
Data collected by private companies is their exclusive intellectual property
Data should be freely shared to promote innovation and economic growth
quizQuestions.explanation: Data ownership as an ethical principle holds that individuals should have meaningful rights to access, correct, and control personal data generated by their activities.

quizQuestions.question: The ethical principle of non-maleficence, applied to AI, requires that developers:
options: Ensure AI systems are always more accurate than human alternatives
Avoid deploying AI systems that could cause foreseeable harm to users or third parties
Prioritize AI development speed over rigorous safety testing
Only develop AI applications that generate profit for developers
quizQuestions.explanation: Non-maleficence (do no harm) in AI ethics requires that developers anticipate, identify, and mitigate foreseeable harms before deployment — especially in high-stakes applications.

quizQuestions.question: Informed consent in medical ethics requires that patients:
options: Agree to any treatment their doctor recommends
Receive complete information about risks and alternatives and voluntarily agree to treatment free from coercion
Sign consent forms prepared by the hospital's legal department
Defer all medical decisions to family members if they are ill
quizQuestions.explanation: Informed consent requires disclosure of material information (diagnosis, treatment options, risks), patient comprehension of that information, and voluntary agreement free from coercion.

quizQuestions.question: The four principles of bioethics developed by Beauchamp and Childress are:
options: Justice, equality, freedom, and dignity
Autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, and justice
Truth, compassion, respect, and accountability
Rights, duties, care, and virtue
quizQuestions.explanation: Beauchamp and Childress's 'principlism' framework identifies four core bioethical principles: respect for autonomy, beneficence (do good), non-maleficence (do no harm), and justice (fair distribution).

quizQuestions.question: The bioethical principle of beneficence requires medical practitioners to:
options: Do nothing that could cause any harm to the patient
Act in the patient's best interest and promote their health and wellbeing
Follow the patient's wishes even when they conflict with medical best practice
Distribute healthcare resources equally among all patients
quizQuestions.explanation: Beneficence is the duty to act in the patient's best interest and promote their well-being — actively doing good rather than merely avoiding harm.

quizQuestions.question: Futile treatment in medical ethics refers to treatment that:
options: Is too expensive for most patients to afford
Provides no reasonable hope of meaningful benefit to the patient
Is not covered by standard health insurance plans
Is considered experimental and not yet approved for standard use
quizQuestions.explanation: Medical futility refers to interventions that have no reasonable chance of providing meaningful benefit — continuing them raises questions of justice (resources), autonomy, and non-maleficence.

quizQuestions.question: The ethical debate over physician-assisted dying centers primarily on the tension between:
options: Patient autonomy and state interests in regulating the medical profession
Patient autonomy and the physician's duty of beneficence and non-maleficence
Medical costs and the healthcare system's need for efficiency
Religious freedom and secular bioethical principles
quizQuestions.explanation: Physician-assisted dying debates pit patient autonomy (the right to choose death) against the physician's traditional duties of beneficence (doing good) and non-maleficence (avoiding harm).

quizQuestions.question: John Rawls's 'veil of ignorance' thought experiment asks us to:
options: Ignore all facts about a moral situation to make objective judgments
Design society's basic structure without knowing our own position, talents, or circumstances in it
Make moral judgments while pretending to be from a different culture
Ignore the consequences of our decisions to focus only on duties
quizQuestions.explanation: The veil of ignorance is a hypothetical device: if we didn't know our place in society (race, class, gender, ability), we would design fair institutions that protect everyone — including the least advantaged.

quizQuestions.question: Rawls's 'difference principle' holds that social and economic inequalities are just only if:
options: All citizens have equal wealth and social status
They benefit the least advantaged members of society as much as possible
They result from free market competition without government interference
All citizens had equal opportunity to compete for better positions
quizQuestions.explanation: The difference principle permits inequalities only when they are arranged to maximally benefit those who are worst off — justifying inequality only when it raises everyone's floor.

quizQuestions.question: Martha Nussbaum's capabilities approach to justice focuses on ensuring that all people have:
options: Equal income and wealth to enable basic consumption
Access to the central capabilities (health, education, political participation, etc.) necessary for a dignified human life
Free market opportunities to pursue any economic goals they choose
Legal equality without substantive attention to material inequalities
quizQuestions.explanation: Nussbaum's capabilities approach argues that justice requires ensuring all people can achieve a minimum threshold of 10 central human capabilities — not just formal rights but real practical ability to live well.

quizQuestions.question: Robert Nozick's libertarian critique of Rawls argues that:
options: Rawls doesn't go far enough in redistributing wealth to the poor
Forced redistribution of legitimately acquired holdings violates individual rights regardless of the outcome
Justice requires everyone to have exactly equal shares of wealth
The government should determine the just distribution of all social goods
quizQuestions.explanation: Nozick's entitlement theory holds that if holdings were acquired justly and transferred voluntarily, redistribution through taxation violates individual property rights — even if it would reduce inequality.

quizQuestions.question: Global justice refers to the question of:
options: Whether international courts can prosecute war crimes effectively
Whether wealthy nations and global institutions have obligations of justice to people in poor countries
Whether international trade agreements are fairly negotiated
Whether global media fairly represents all nations
quizQuestions.explanation: Global justice asks whether our moral obligations of fairness — to reduce poverty, respect rights, address climate change — extend beyond national borders to people in other countries.

quizQuestions.question: The first step in most systematic ethical decision-making frameworks is to:
options: Select the ethical theory that supports your preferred conclusion
Clearly identify and define the ethical dilemma or problem
Immediately act on your initial moral intuition
Consult the relevant legal statutes before anything else
quizQuestions.explanation: Ethical decision-making frameworks consistently begin with problem identification — clearly defining the ethical issue, who is affected, and what is at stake.

quizQuestions.question: Why is identifying stakeholders important in ethical decision-making?
options: To determine who has legal liability for the outcome
To ensure all parties who will be affected by the decision are considered
To find out who has the most power to influence the outcome
To identify who should make the final decision
quizQuestions.explanation: Stakeholder identification ensures that the interests and rights of all parties affected by a decision are considered — not just the decision-maker's interests or the most powerful parties.

quizQuestions.question: Using multiple ethical frameworks (utilitarian, deontological, virtue) in a decision is useful because:
options: One framework is always clearly correct for any given situation
Different frameworks highlight different morally relevant considerations, providing a more complete analysis
It allows decision-makers to choose whichever conclusion they prefer
It satisfies legal requirements for ethical decision documentation
quizQuestions.explanation: Applying multiple frameworks reveals different morally relevant dimensions of a situation — consequences, duties, character — producing more comprehensive analysis than any single framework alone.

quizQuestions.question: Moral intuition in ethical decision-making should be:
options: Always followed without question as the truest moral guide
Always overridden by theoretical ethical reasoning
Taken seriously as data but subjected to critical reflection and refinement
Ignored entirely in favor of systematic theoretical analysis
quizQuestions.explanation: Strong moral intuitions represent accumulated moral wisdom and should be taken seriously as evidence; but intuitions can also be mistaken, so they need critical reflection and dialogue to refine them.

quizQuestions.question: The final step of most ethical decision-making frameworks involves:
options: Ensuring the decision maximizes financial benefit for all parties
Evaluating the decision — assessing whether the outcome was right and what you learned
Documenting the decision for legal protection
Getting approval from a supervisor before implementing the decision
quizQuestions.explanation: Ethical decision-making frameworks close with evaluation — reflecting on outcomes, whether the decision was well-reasoned, what you would do differently, and what moral lessons the experience offers.

quizQuestions.question: Professional ethics refers to:
options: The personal moral beliefs of individuals in a profession
The special ethical obligations and standards that apply to practitioners of a specific profession
Government regulations that all businesses must follow
The informal social norms of a workplace culture
quizQuestions.explanation: Professional ethics encompasses the specific moral duties, codes of conduct, and ethical standards that govern the behavior of practitioners in a defined field or profession.

quizQuestions.question: The physician's duty of confidentiality is grounded primarily in the ethical principle of:
options: Beneficence — protecting patients' health interests
Respect for autonomy — protecting the patient's right to control their own private information
Justice — ensuring equal treatment for all patients
Non-maleficence — preventing harm to third parties who might misuse the information
quizQuestions.explanation: Medical confidentiality is primarily grounded in patient autonomy — the right to control sensitive personal information — though it also serves beneficence by enabling honest communication.

quizQuestions.question: Whistleblowing in a professional context involves:
options: Reporting a competitor's illegal behavior to regulatory authorities
Disclosing organizational misconduct to authorities or the public, often at personal risk
Using media contacts to resolve internal workplace disputes
Blowing the final whistle on a failed negotiation between parties
quizQuestions.explanation: Whistleblowing involves disclosing internal organizational wrongdoing (fraud, safety violations, ethical misconduct) to external authorities or the public, often at significant personal professional risk.

quizQuestions.question: Conflicts of interest in professional life occur when:
options: Two colleagues in the same profession disagree about ethical standards
A personal interest could compromise or appear to compromise the professional's objective judgment
A professional is required to work for two competing clients simultaneously
Professional standards conflict with current government regulations
quizQuestions.explanation: A conflict of interest exists when a professional's personal interests (financial, personal relationships) could influence — or appear to influence — their professional judgment and duties.

quizQuestions.question: The fiduciary duty in professional ethics requires professionals to:
options: Maximize their own financial compensation for services rendered
Act primarily in the client's or patient's best interest, placing their interests above the professional's own
Follow all regulations even when they conflict with client interests
Disclose all professional communications to regulatory bodies
quizQuestions.explanation: A fiduciary duty is the highest legal and ethical standard of care — requiring the professional (lawyer, doctor, financial advisor) to act exclusively in the client's best interest.

questions.question: The fundamental question that normative ethics attempts to answer is:
options: How do people actually behave in moral situations?
What actions, practices, or values are morally right or good?
What is the historical origin of our moral beliefs?
How can we scientifically measure the morality of actions?
questions.explanation: Normative ethics is prescriptive — it asks what we ought to do, what is good, and what principles should guide moral decisions.

questions.question: Utilitarianism evaluates the morality of an action by:
options: The intentions of the person performing the action
The overall consequences — whether it maximizes happiness and minimizes suffering
Whether the action conforms to universal moral duties
The character of the person who performs the action
questions.explanation: Utilitarianism is a consequentialist theory — the right action is whichever produces the greatest total well-being for all affected parties.

questions.question: Kant's categorical imperative differs from utilitarianism primarily in that it:
options: Allows for exceptions when consequences are severe enough
Provides absolute duties that apply regardless of consequences
Requires considering the happiness of all affected parties
Bases morality on the virtuous character of the moral agent
questions.explanation: Kant's ethics is deontological — moral duties apply categorically and unconditionally, regardless of whether following them produces better or worse consequences.

questions.question: Aristotle's concept of eudaimonia refers to:
options: The maximum amount of pleasure a person can experience
Human flourishing through the excellent exercise of our characteristic rational capacities
Complete freedom from all suffering and pain
Fulfilling all of one's social and family obligations
questions.explanation: Eudaimonia is the excellent, flourishing human life achieved by developing and exercising virtues — the qualities that enable us to live and act well as rational, social beings.

questions.question: The strongest critique of moral relativism is that it:
options: Makes cross-cultural communication impossible
Cannot coherently condemn clear atrocities if the perpetrating culture approves of them
Is too tolerant of cultural differences that should be accepted
Requires perfect knowledge of all cultural norms before making judgments
questions.explanation: Moral relativism logically prevents condemning genocide, torture, or slavery if the perpetrating society approves — a conclusion most find morally unacceptable.

questions.question: A valid argument is one in which:
options: All premises are factually true
The conclusion necessarily follows from the premises
Both premises and conclusion are true
The argument is rhetorically convincing to its audience
questions.explanation: Validity is purely structural: if the premises were true, the conclusion would have to be true. An argument can be valid even with false premises.

questions.question: The ad hominem fallacy attacks the ______ rather than the argument.
options: Evidence used in the argument
Person making the argument
Logic of the argument's structure
Conclusion of the argument
questions.explanation: Ad hominem ('against the person') attempts to refute an argument by attacking the character, identity, or circumstances of the arguer rather than addressing the argument's actual merits.

questions.question: Confirmation bias leads people to evaluate evidence:
options: More carefully when it supports their prior beliefs
Favorably when it confirms existing beliefs and dismissively when it challenges them
More critically when it comes from reputable academic sources
Consistently regardless of whether it supports or challenges their beliefs
questions.explanation: Confirmation bias systematically distorts evidence evaluation — people accept confirming evidence uncritically while subjecting disconfirming evidence to excessive scrutiny.

questions.question: Kant's Formula of Humanity requires that we never:
options: Tell a lie under any circumstances
Use other people merely as tools or instruments for our own ends
Act in ways that maximize collective welfare
Break social contracts without the consent of all parties
questions.explanation: The Formula of Humanity requires treating every rational being as an end in themselves — never merely as a means to our own purposes — because rational beings have inherent dignity.

questions.question: The doctrine of the mean in virtue ethics means that virtues are:
options: Exactly the average of what most people do in society
Appropriate dispositions that avoid both excess and deficiency in character and action
Moderate, cautious positions that avoid extreme claims
Behaviors that produce the most average amount of happiness
questions.explanation: Aristotle's mean is not a mathematical average but an appropriate response — courage, for example, lies between the extremes of cowardice (deficiency) and recklessness (excess).

questions.question: The Universal Declaration of Human Rights reflects which moral approach?
options: Moral relativism — different standards for different cultures
Moral universalism — rights that apply to all humans everywhere
Virtue ethics — rights earned through virtuous conduct
Rule utilitarianism — rights that generally maximize welfare
questions.explanation: The UDHR is grounded in moral universalism — the belief that all humans possess certain rights by virtue of their humanity, which no cultural or state authority can legitimately override.

questions.question: Post hoc ergo propter hoc is the fallacy of assuming:
options: What most people believe must be true
Because B came after A, A must have caused B
One's position must be correct because one has held it for a long time
That the burden of proof rests with those challenging a traditional view
questions.explanation: Post hoc ('after this, therefore because of this') commits the error of inferring causation from mere temporal sequence — the fact that B followed A does not prove A caused B.

questions.question: Anecdotal evidence is considered weak primarily because:
options: It is usually fabricated rather than based on real experience
A single case cannot reliably generalize to a broader population
It is emotional rather than rational in its appeal
It is not accepted by academic journals in any discipline
questions.explanation: Individual anecdotes may be true but cannot reliably represent the full range of cases — an exceptional personal experience may be atypical rather than representative of the broader pattern.

questions.question: The straw man fallacy involves misrepresenting an opponent's position to:
options: Make it more nuanced than it actually is
Make it easier to attack than the actual position
Show that the opponent has no consistent position
Demonstrate that the opponent is arguing in bad faith
questions.explanation: The straw man replaces an opponent's actual position with a weakened or distorted version, then attacks that weaker version — creating the illusion of refuting the original argument.

questions.question: Mill's modification to Bentham's utilitarianism distinguished between 'higher' and 'lower' pleasures because he argued that:
options: Rich people's pleasures should count more in utilitarian calculations
Intellectual and moral pleasures are qualitatively superior to physical pleasures
Only pleasures that society approves should be counted
The intensity of pleasure is the only morally relevant factor
questions.explanation: Mill argued that not all pleasures are equal in quality — intellectual, artistic, and moral pleasures are qualitatively superior to mere physical satisfaction, even at lower intensity.

questions.question: Rule utilitarianism differs from act utilitarianism by evaluating actions based on:
options: The actual consequences of the specific action
Whether the action conforms to a rule that generally maximizes utility if universally followed
The character and intentions of the person performing the action
The majority opinion about whether the action is good
questions.explanation: Rule utilitarianism judges actions by whether they follow rules (keep promises, don't lie) that generally produce better outcomes when followed universally — avoiding some objections to act utilitarianism.

questions.question: Kant's Formula of Universal Law tests the morality of an action by asking:
options: Does this action produce more good than harm for everyone affected?
Could I will that everyone act on the same maxim without contradiction?
Would a virtuous person of good character perform this action?
Does this action violate any existing laws or social norms?
questions.explanation: The Formula of Universal Law: universalize the maxim behind your action — if doing so leads to contradiction or a world no rational being could want, the action is wrong.

questions.question: Virtue ethics focuses on the development of character over time primarily because:
options: Rules and consequences are never reliable guides to action
Being a good person with good character naturally leads to right actions across diverse situations
Character determines legal standing in civil and criminal courts
Virtuous character is inherited rather than developed
questions.explanation: Virtue ethics holds that rules and calculations are insufficient — a person of good character (courageous, honest, just) will perceive what situations require and respond appropriately.

questions.question: The environmental ethics concept of intrinsic value holds that nature:
options: Has value only as a resource for human economic activity
Has value in itself, independent of its usefulness to humans
Has value determined by how much money humans pay for it
Has equal moral status to all human individuals
questions.explanation: Intrinsic value means something is valuable in and of itself — not merely because of its instrumental usefulness to humans. Assigning intrinsic value to nature grounds stronger environmental obligations.

questions.question: Peter Singer's utilitarian argument for animal rights rests on the claim that:
options: Animals have the same legal rights as humans in most countries
The capacity to suffer — not species membership — is the morally relevant criterion for moral consideration
All living organisms deserve equal moral protection regardless of sentience
Animal welfare should be considered only when it does not conflict with human interests
questions.explanation: Singer argues speciesism is morally arbitrary — if suffering matters morally, then animal suffering should matter equally to equivalent human suffering.

questions.question: The four principles of bioethics (Beauchamp and Childress) most directly conflict when:
options: A patient from a different cultural background receives treatment
Respecting patient autonomy conflicts with the physician's duty to prevent harm
Two different physicians have different treatment recommendations
Insurance companies deny coverage for a recommended treatment
questions.explanation: The classic tension in bioethics is between autonomy (patient's right to choose) and non-maleficence/beneficence (physician's duty not to harm and to help) — most evident in cases like refusal of life-saving treatment.

questions.question: Rawls's veil of ignorance is intended to produce:
options: Decision-makers who are ignorant of all facts about a situation
Principles of justice that are fair because they are chosen without self-interested advantage
A society where all individuals are literally equal in wealth and opportunity
Ethical judgments free from any influence of emotion or intuition
questions.explanation: The veil of ignorance is a fairness device: principles chosen without knowing your own social position cannot favor your group over others — ensuring fairness by removing self-interested bias.

questions.question: Algorithmic bias in AI is most ethically concerning because it:
options: Makes AI systems less profitable for the companies that develop them
Can systematically disadvantage already marginalized groups in consequential, high-stakes decisions
Reveals that AI can never be truly objective in any application
Requires expensive retraining that most companies cannot afford
questions.explanation: Algorithmic bias is a justice issue — it reproduces and often amplifies existing social inequities in hiring, lending, criminal sentencing, and other high-stakes domains where bias causes serious real-world harm.

questions.question: Applying multiple ethical frameworks to a moral dilemma is valuable because:
options: It allows decision-makers to select whichever conclusion they prefer
Different frameworks highlight different morally relevant considerations that a single framework might miss
It always produces a clear consensus answer that all reasonable people will accept
It satisfies legal requirements for ethical decision documentation
questions.explanation: Multi-framework analysis reveals that utilitarian, deontological, and virtue considerations each contribute something different — together they produce more complete moral analysis than any single lens.

questions.question: Professional whistleblowing is ethically difficult primarily because it:
options: Is always illegal under corporate confidentiality agreements
Requires risking personal professional harm to serve a public good
Violates the whistle-blower's duty of loyalty to their employer
Is only justified when the wrong being reported causes direct physical harm
questions.explanation: Whistleblowing creates genuine ethical tension between loyalty to colleagues and employer, professional confidentiality, and the obligation to prevent or disclose wrongdoing — at significant personal risk.

questions.question: The precautionary principle in environmental ethics is most applicable when:
options: Scientific research on an issue has produced a clear, definitive consensus
There is scientific uncertainty but evidence of potential serious or irreversible harm
Proposed actions have well-documented track records of safety and efficacy
Economic benefits of an action clearly outweigh potential environmental costs
questions.explanation: The precautionary principle calls for preventive action in the face of scientific uncertainty about serious harm — particularly for irreversible environmental damage where we cannot undo mistakes.

questions.question: The correlation-causation distinction is important in ethical reasoning because:
options: Correlations are always statistically stronger than causal relationships
Mistaking correlation for causation can lead to ineffective or harmful policy interventions
Only causal evidence is admissible in ethical arguments
Correlational research is conducted by less credible researchers
questions.explanation: Misidentifying a correlation as causal leads to wrong solutions — targeting the symptom rather than the cause, wasting resources, and potentially causing harm through misguided intervention.

questions.question: The false dilemma fallacy is problematic in ethical reasoning because it:
options: Creates too many options for decision-makers to evaluate clearly
Artificially narrows the choice space, preventing consideration of better alternatives
Combines two different ethical theories inappropriately
Presents only the most extreme positions on both sides of an issue
questions.explanation: False dilemmas constrain reasoning by presenting only two options when more exist — leading decision-makers to choose between bad options when a better alternative is available.

questions.question: Rawls's difference principle justifies economic inequality only when inequalities:
options: Result from individual talent and hard work rather than inheritance
Benefit the least advantaged members of society as much as possible
Are the result of free market competition without government interference
Are consistent with equal opportunity for all citizens to compete
questions.explanation: The difference principle allows departures from equality only when those inequalities genuinely benefit the least advantaged — making the worst-off members as well-off as possible.

questions.question: The fiduciary duty in professional ethics is most directly grounded in which ethical principle?
options: Utilitarian maximization of the professional's own welfare
Unconditional prioritization of the client's interests over the professional's own interests
Equal treatment of all clients regardless of their individual needs
Following legal regulations even when they conflict with client interests
questions.explanation: Fiduciary duty — the highest standard of professional care — requires placing the client's interests first, unconditionally, above the professional's own financial or personal interests.

questions.question: Moral intuitions are best treated in ethical reasoning as:
options: Absolute moral truths that override all theoretical arguments
Significant data points that should be taken seriously but subjected to critical reflection
Irrelevant subjective feelings that ethical reasoning must eliminate
Direct windows into objective moral truth that require no further analysis
questions.explanation: Strong moral intuitions carry evidential weight — they reflect accumulated moral experience — but they can also reflect bias, culture, or limited perspective, requiring critical reflection.

questions.question: Nozick's libertarian challenge to redistributive justice argues that:
options: Redistribution should be much more aggressive than Rawls recommends
Taxing legitimately acquired holdings to redistribute wealth violates individual property rights
Government has no role in ensuring any form of social welfare
Free markets always produce just distributions without any need for regulation
questions.explanation: Nozick's entitlement theory holds that if holdings were acquired and transferred justly, government redistribution — even for egalitarian goals — violates the right-holders' individual rights.

questions.question: Martha Nussbaum's capabilities approach argues that justice requires ensuring people can:
options: Purchase any goods and services available in a free market
Actually achieve a minimum threshold of central human capabilities needed for a dignified life
Earn an equal income regardless of their chosen occupation
Access information about all available life choices freely
questions.explanation: Nussbaum's approach focuses on real human capabilities — the practical ability to be healthy, educated, politically engaged — not just formal rights or income equality.

questions.question: AI transparency is an ethical requirement primarily because:
options: Transparent AI systems are always more accurate than opaque ones
People affected by AI decisions deserve to understand and be able to contest those decisions
Transparency allows competitors to copy successful AI systems
Government regulations in all countries require AI code to be publicly available
questions.explanation: AI transparency is fundamentally about accountability and autonomy — people whose lives are affected by algorithmic decisions should be able to understand the basis for those decisions and have recourse.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes the relationship between ethics and law?
options: Law and ethics are identical — legal equals moral
Ethics provides the moral foundations and justifications for law, but legal and ethical requirements can diverge
Law determines what is ethical — whatever is legal is automatically moral
Ethics applies only to private life while law governs public behavior
questions.explanation: Ethics and law are related but distinct — ethics provides the moral foundations for law, but historically many laws have been unethical (slavery, apartheid) and some ethical obligations exceed legal requirements.
